title: Meridian Series A Pitch Deck — Technical Architecture
extracted_text:
MERIDIAN PLATFORM — TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE OVERVIEW
Prepared for Series A investors. Author: Alice (CTO), Eve (CEO).

EXECUTIVE SUMMARY
Meridian has built a production-grade data platform on Postgres, Kafka, React/TypeScript, and Kubernetes on GCP. Every technical decision was made for reliability and scalability at enterprise scale.

CORE PLATFORM COMPONENTS

1. ATLAS — Transaction Ledger (Lead: Alice)
Database: PostgreSQL — chosen for ACID compliance. MongoDB was evaluated and rejected due to eventual consistency limitations that are unacceptable for financial transaction data. Atlas handles 12,000 transactions per second in production. The Postgres schema uses UUID primary keys, JSONB for flexible metadata, and comprehensive indexing. PgBouncer connection pooling at 100 connections max. Redis caching layer reduces Postgres load by 40%.

2. PHOENIX — Event Streaming (Lead: Bob)
Framework: Apache Kafka — migrated from RabbitMQ in Q1 2024. RabbitMQ was hitting its ceiling at 80,000 messages per second. Kafka now handles 600,000 messages per second with 3 brokers in KRaft mode on Kubernetes. Topics: atlas-events (24 partitions), orion-predictions (12 partitions), horizon-notifications (6 partitions). All brokers on Titan Kubernetes cluster on GCP.

3. ORION — ML Predictions (Lead: Dave)
Framework: PyTorch — chosen over TensorFlow for dynamic computation graph and team expertise. Training on GCP T4 GPU instances, orchestrated by Airflow. Inference via FastAPI: 58ms p99 latency. 96% prediction accuracy on Atlas production data. Vector storage: Pinecone. Q3 roadmap: LangChain integration for natural language queries.

4. HORIZON — Customer Dashboard (Lead: Carol)
Stack: React + TypeScript — standardized on TypeScript strict mode in Q1 2024 after maintenance issues with mixed JS/TS. GraphQL external API, REST internal APIs, Kafka WebSockets for real-time updates. All 47 UI components typed and documented in Storybook. Design system: Radix UI primitives with custom semantic tokens.

5. TITAN — Infrastructure (Lead: Frank)
Orchestration: Kubernetes on GCP (GKE Autopilot). IaC: Terraform. Containers: Docker. Observability: Prometheus + Grafana + Sentry + Datadog. Secrets: GitHub Secrets, rotated quarterly. Cost optimization: GCP enterprise agreement reduces compute spend by 30%.

COMPETITIVE DIFFERENTIATION
Our competitors use MongoDB for their transaction data — this has caused publicized data consistency issues for their customers. Our Postgres-based Atlas ledger with ACID compliance is our primary enterprise differentiator, as validated by 18 of 20 customer interviews conducted by Blake and Eve.
author: alice

title: Meridian Technical Specification — Atlas v1.0
extracted_text:
ATLAS TRANSACTION LEDGER — TECHNICAL SPECIFICATION v1.0
Classification: Internal | Author: Alice | Reviewed by: Bob, Frank

1. OVERVIEW
Atlas is the core transaction ledger for the Meridian platform. It provides ACID-compliant transaction processing backed by PostgreSQL. This specification covers the v1.0 architecture as deployed in production in Q1 2024.

2. TECHNOLOGY DECISIONS

2.1 Database: PostgreSQL
Postgres was chosen after a two-week evaluation against MongoDB in January 2024 (led by Alice). The decision criteria:
- ACID compliance: REQUIRED. Postgres delivers. MongoDB rejected for eventual consistency.
- Write throughput: 10k+ TPS required. Postgres with PgBouncer delivers 12k TPS in production.
- Replication: Postgres streaming replication to read replica for Horizon and Prism.
- Extensions: JSONB for metadata, pg_stat_statements for query analysis.

2.2 Caching: Redis
Redis handles all hot read paths — account balance caching, session state, and feature flags for Horizon. Frank provisioned the Redis cluster on GCP. Cache hit rate: 85%+ on account balance queries.

2.3 Search: Elasticsearch
Elasticsearch provides full-text search over the Atlas transaction history. MongoDB's text search was evaluated and rejected (Elasticsearch gives better relevance scoring). Dave integrated Elasticsearch. Alice reviewed the index design.

2.4 Event Streaming: Kafka (Phoenix)
All Atlas writes emit events to the Phoenix Kafka topic (atlas-events, 24 partitions). This enables: Orion ML training data, Horizon real-time notifications, Prism analytics aggregates, Apex export triggers. Bob designed and owns the Kafka integration.

3. API DESIGN
External API: GraphQL (chosen by Carol after evaluation of REST-only approach). Internal APIs: REST via FastAPI. Authentication: OAuth + JWT (validated by the Nexus API gateway). Both REST and GraphQL are documented in Avery's API documentation.

4. PERFORMANCE TARGETS
v1.0 (current): 12k TPS, p99 latency < 5ms, 99.99% uptime SLA.
v2.0 (Q2 target): 50k TPS with Elasticsearch, connection pool optimization, read replica scaling.
v3.0 (Q3 stretch): 100k TPS with Vega OLAP offloading long-running analytical queries from Atlas Postgres.

5. OPERATIONS
Owner: Alice (primary), Grace (secondary).
On-call: PagerDuty. Atlas transaction failures or Postgres replication lag alerts route to Alice and Frank immediately.
Monitoring: Prometheus metrics (Grafana dashboards), Sentry error tracking, Datadog APM for query performance.
author: alice

title: Meridian Engineering Onboarding Guide
extracted_text:
MERIDIAN ENGINEERING ONBOARDING — NEW ENGINEER GUIDE
Authors: Dave and Frank | Last updated: March 2024

WELCOME TO MERIDIAN ENGINEERING
This guide covers everything you need to get up and running on the Meridian platform. The monorepo is on GitHub. All services use Docker for local development.

PLATFORM OVERVIEW
Meridian's platform has five core services:
- Atlas: Transaction ledger (Postgres + FastAPI). Lead: Alice.
- Phoenix: Event streaming (Kafka + consumers). Lead: Bob.
- Orion: ML predictions (PyTorch + FastAPI). Lead: Dave.
- Horizon: Customer dashboard (React + TypeScript + GraphQL). Lead: Carol.
- Titan: Infrastructure (Kubernetes + Terraform on GCP). Lead: Frank.

KEY TECHNICAL DECISIONS TO KNOW
1. We use Postgres for Atlas — not MongoDB. This was a deliberate decision for ACID compliance made by Alice in January 2024. If you're coming from a MongoDB background, the ACID model is a core requirement — don't propose MongoDB for any Atlas data.
2. We use Kafka for Phoenix — migrated from RabbitMQ by Bob in Q1 2024. Kafka handles 600k msg/s. All service integration uses Kafka topics, not direct API calls between services.
3. We use TypeScript strict mode for all frontend code — Carol standardized this in Q1 2024. No `any` types. The CI pipeline blocks on TypeScript errors.
4. We use PyTorch for Orion — Dave's decision. All ML work goes through the Airflow pipeline on GCP. Model artifacts in Google Cloud Storage.
5. We use GCP and Kubernetes for everything — Frank's infrastructure decisions. All Docker containers, Terraform for infra, GKE Autopilot for scheduling.

LOCAL DEVELOPMENT SETUP
1. Clone the GitHub monorepo
2. Install Docker Desktop
3. Run `docker-compose up` to start: Postgres (Atlas), Kafka (Phoenix), Redis (cache), Elasticsearch (search)
4. Environment variables: copy `.env.example` to `.env`. Ask Frank for the GCP credentials and JWT secrets.
5. Run `pnpm dev` in the Horizon directory for the React TypeScript frontend
6. For Orion: you need Python 3.11 and PyTorch. Dave can provision a GCP instance for GPU training.

KEY CONTACTS
- Alice: Atlas architecture questions, Postgres performance, ACID requirements
- Bob: Phoenix Kafka topics, consumer group configuration, event streaming design
- Carol: Horizon React TypeScript, GraphQL API, design system questions
- Dave: Orion PyTorch models, Airflow pipelines, GCP ML infrastructure
- Frank: Titan Kubernetes, Terraform, Docker, GCP access, secrets management
- Eve: Product strategy, roadmap, OKRs
- Quinn: Recruiting and HR
- Rachel: Legal, security, compliance, GDPR
author: dave
